answer:
**Allama Iqbal's poem "Wataniyat" (Nationalism)** appears in *Bang-E-Dara Part 3* (1924), where he critiques territorial nationalism as a modern idol that undermines Islamic unity and faith.[^1]

> Nationalism[^2]  
>   
> In this new age, the wine, the cup, the Jam are new,[^3]  
> The Saqi’s ways of grace and wrath are fashioned new.  
> The Muslim, too, has built his sanctum all anew,  
> While Civilization’s idol-smith carves idols new.  
> Of all these newer gods, the greatest is the Nation;  
> The robe it wears is Faith’s own final desecration.[^4]  
>   
> This idol, forged by this new age’s philosophy,[^5]  
> Lays waste the Prophet’s house of faith and piety.  
> Your arm is mighty with the strength of Oneness still,  
> Your homeland is Islam, you serve the Prophet’s will.  
> O Mustafavi, show the world that ancient sight!  
> And grind this hollow idol into dust and night![^6]  
>   
> To be confined to one place is calamity,[^7]  
> Live free within the ocean, like a fish at sea.  
> To leave one’s home is the Beloved’s sacred way,  
> So bear you witness to the Prophet’s truth today.  
> In the discourse of the state, the Nation is but land;  
> In the Prophet’s teaching, Homeland is by God’s command.[^8]  
>   
> Among the world’s great peoples, rivalry it sows,[^9]  
> The aim of trade becomes conquest where’er it goes.  
> From it, all politics from truth and candor flees,  
> And by its hand the weak man’s home is brought to its knees.  
> By it, God’s own creation is in pieces torn,  
> By it, the roots of Islam’s brotherhood are shorn.[^10]

Iqbal portrays nationalism (*wataniyat*) as a false deity that fragments the universal Muslim *ummah*, contrasting it with the Prophet's vision of faith transcending borders.[^1]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [chapter 25](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25/translation/).

[^2]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [p-3b9de1](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25/translation/#p-3b9de1).

[^3]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [p-79b1e9](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25/translation/#p-79b1e9).

[^4]: Ibid., [p-79b1e9](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25/translation/#p-79b1e9).

[^5]: Ibid., [p-2737d5](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25/translation/#p-2737d5).

[^6]: Ibid.

[^7]: Ibid., [p-a723b2](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25/translation/#p-a723b2).

[^8]: Ibid.

[^9]: Ibid., [p-97937b](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25/translation/#p-97937b).

[^10]: Ibid.

*(Note: I reconstructed the citation URLs based on the standard format from the corpus tools: `/works/{work_slug}/{chapter_number}/{variant}/#p-{paragraph_id}`. The poem is presented stanza-by-stanza matching the paragraph breaks in the translation variant. Original Urdu, transliteration, and full context are available in the linked chapter.)*
